identity.title: Rooming and Boarding Houses, Dormitories, and Workers' Camps

lens.included_activities: US operators of rooming and boarding houses, off-campus dormitories, and worker accommodations that repeatedly provide managed lodging and related services to paying residents or external institutional or employer customers, with normalized EBITDA of roughly $1-10 million.
lens.excluded_activities: Passive real-estate ownership without operating staff; captive on-campus dormitories and employer-run camps serving only the owner's own organization; fraternity, sorority, religious, or other membership housing that is not commercially transferable; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Residents commonly pay recurring bed or room rent under monthly or academic-year arrangements; student housing may be leased by the bed, while externally operated worker housing may be paid by an employer or project sponsor. Housekeeping, meals, laundry, maintenance, and resident support may be bundled or sold as complementary services.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is heterogeneous across resident-paid rooming houses, student housing, membership housing, and worker camps. To keep the screen coherent, it retains only commercially transferable operators serving external residents or sponsors and excludes captive or nontransferable organization housing; this is a business-model coherence restriction, not an attractiveness selection.

a.rationale: The estimate is built from the industry occupation mix rather than a generic AI index. In May 2023, food-service occupations were 21.47% of employment, building cleaning and grounds 27.21%, residential advisors 13.19%, and installation/maintenance 7.25%; these are mostly physical or in-person. Office and administrative support was 15.22%, management 9.08%, and business/financial work 2.46%, providing the main wage-weighted exposure. Within the 9.59% desk-clerk occupation, reservations, account records, charges, inquiries, and bookkeeping are digitally tractable, while greeting, escalation, key control, and local coordination remain human. Allowing modest exposure in supervision, scheduling, resident communications, procurement, and documentation produces a base near one-fifth of wages.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary from 20-30 eligible operators, mapped to O*NET tasks and split between already automated and not-yet-automated work, would materially move this estimate; property-management-system logs showing labor minutes by reservation, billing, resident-support, housekeeping, and maintenance workflow would be especially useful.
caveats: OEWS covers all employer establishments in NAICS 721300 and excludes self-employed workers; it is not limited to the eligible LMM lens.
The estimate applies judgment to task exposure and does not assume that all clerical work is substitutable.
Occupation shares are employment-weighted in the source; the estimate adjusts qualitatively for wage differences rather than reconstructing microdata-level wage weights.

rho.bridge: The Census BTOS proxy measures current and near-term AI use across all US businesses, not the five-year implementation share of exposed work in NAICS 721310; the customer-support field study measures productivity from one conversational-assistance deployment, not substitution in lodging. I use the observed 17%-20% current business adoption and 20%-23% six-month expectation as a lower-anchor on diffusion, then raise the five-year base because reservations, billing, communications, and scheduling already run in software. This can overstate implementation if small lodging operators lack integration capacity, and understate it if bundled property-management vendors diffuse AI rapidly.
rho.rationale: Implementation should be meaningful but incomplete. Census reported economy-wide AI use around 17%-20% from December 2025 through May 2026, less than 20% among firms with four or fewer employees, and materially higher use at larger firms. A field deployment among 5,179 support agents improved issues resolved per hour by 14%, demonstrating implementability for customer-contact work. Eligible operators still face fragmented property systems, privacy and fair-housing controls, identity and payment risk, exception-heavy resident service, and 24/7 accountability, so only a little over half of the exposed opportunity is expected to be operational within five years in the base case.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor-level adoption and realized-labor data from property-management platforms serving boarding, student, or workforce housing, plus before/after staffing records from at least 10 deployments over 12 months, would materially change this range. The needed fields are eligible labor hours, automated workflow share, human override rates, quality incidents, and net headcount or avoided hires.
caveats: BTOS changed its AI-use wording in late 2025 and is economy-wide rather than industry-specific.
The field study concerns assisted customer support, not rooming-house operations or full task substitution.
Implementation is sensitive to operator size and the quality of incumbent property-management data.

e.rationale: Census defines the code to include rooming houses, off-campus dormitories, residential clubs, fraternity and sorority houses, and workers' camps, and reports 1,589 employer establishments in 2023. The lens excludes captive, membership, nonprofit-like nontransferable, and passive-property structures, while retaining resident-paid and externally contracted operating businesses. At the LMM EBITDA band, a commercially managed operating platform is more likely than in the full establishment population, but the mixed code and real-estate-heavy ownership structures justify a base eligibility share only modestly above one-half.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 88 dataset-implied LMM firms using Capital IQ, PitchBook, state lodging licenses, IPEDS ownership links, and operator websites could classify each as external commercial operator, captive/membership, passive property owner, nonprofit, or shell and materially replace this estimate.
caveats: The Census establishment count is not the dataset firm count and does not reveal ownership, EBITDA, captive status, or transferability.
The SEC example demonstrates a transferable commercial student-housing model but is not representative of every component of NAICS 721310.
Eligibility may be lower if many LMM entities are property-holding vehicles rather than staffed operating companies.

s5.rationale: No source located measures five-year qualifying control transfers for eligible NAICS 721310 LMM operators. The national small-business market is active—BizBuySell analyzes roughly 50,000 listed and recently sold businesses and reported 2,345 closed transactions in 2026 Q1—but its transactions are voluntary broker reports, its denominator is not the eligible firm population, and its typical deal is much smaller than the specified EBITDA band. The range therefore treats turnover as a bounded judgment, allowing for owner succession and property-backed acquisitions while discounting asset-only sales, captive reorganizations, and transfers without a continuing operating platform.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction search in PitchBook, Capital IQ, CoStar, MSCI Real Assets, and county deed records, matched to the eligible-firm list and coded for control change versus asset-only sale, would materially change this estimate. Structured interviews with student-housing and workforce-housing brokers could identify unannounced transfers and failed processes.
caveats: The cited market covers US small businesses broadly, not this NAICS code or the $1-10 million EBITDA band.
Real-estate transfers may not constitute a qualifying transfer of an operating business.
Owner age, succession intention, and actual five-year transaction incidence are not observed for the eligible population.

q.rationale: A public-company filing describes student housing leased by the bed on fixed terms aligned to the academic year and billed monthly. That structure does not mechanically pass a labor saving to the resident during the lease, so most near-term benefit can remain with the operator. Annual reletting, competitive rent setting, institutional procurement, service-quality expectations, and the possibility that sponsors negotiate efficiency sharing erode retention over five years. The base therefore retains about two-thirds of an implemented gross benefit on a discounted basis, with lower retention for competitive employer contracts and higher retention where rents are market-set and savings are operationally invisible.
q.change_evidence: Review 30 representative resident leases and employer master-service agreements, including renewal, benchmarking, open-book cost, most-favored-customer, and gain-sharing clauses; then compare renewal pricing and contribution margins before and after automation. This contract-level evidence could materially change the range.
caveats: The filing describes one student-housing property before its sale and does not cover rooming houses or worker-camp contracts.
The estimate separates retention from implementation and demand effects, but competitive responses over five years remain unobserved.
Property rent may reflect location and capital more than service cost, making pass-through weaker than in open-book service contracts.

d5.bridge: NCES projected total degree-granting postsecondary enrollment from 19.808 million in 2026 to 20.234 million in 2031, a ratio of about 1.02; this is a demand-driver proxy for the student-housing component, not constant-price demand for all NAICS 721310 services. BLS projects lodging-manager employment across the broader lodging occupation to grow 3% from 2024 to 2034, while the BLS-derived rooming-house employment index was only 77.0 in 2024 relative to 2017=100, providing mixed context. I anchor the base to the student enrollment ratio and widen the range for worker-camp cycles, rooming-house affordability demand, online education, and component weights. This may overstate demand if off-campus dorm use falls faster than enrollment and understate it if housing shortages or project-related worker accommodation grows.
d5.rationale: The five-year base is approximately flat to slightly growing. Student enrollment, a major plausible driver, is projected to rise about 2% from 2026 to 2031. The broader lodging-management outlook is modestly positive, but historical employment in the specific rooming-house series remains well below its 2017 index level. Constant-quality demand could decline with demographic pressure, remote study, or project-cycle weakness, while housing scarcity and geographically concentrated construction, energy, or infrastructure work support the upside.
d5.change_evidence: Revenue or occupied-bed histories by NAICS component from Census product-line data, IPEDS off-campus bed demand, and commercial workforce-housing utilization data would materially improve this estimate. A five-year contracted-bed pipeline and same-property occupancy series from eligible operators would be decisive.
caveats: NCES projections were prepared in December 2022 and end in 2031; they do not measure housing propensity or the worker-camp and rooming-house components.
The lodging-manager outlook is occupation-wide and mainly reflects traditional hotels and motels.
The employment index is not a direct quantity-demand series and can move with productivity or occupational classification.

o.rationale: The service basket is anchored in physical accommodation and complementary housekeeping, meals, laundry, maintenance, safety, and resident support. Census describes the accommodations as possible principal residences, while O*NET and BLS show physical inspection, cleanliness, key access, payment, complaint resolution, staff coordination, and around-the-clock accountability. Software can enable self-service check-in, messaging, billing, and triage, but it is unlikely to eliminate the need for an accountable operator at occupied facilities within five years. The base allows limited displacement by resident self-service or software-only administration while preserving nearly all operator-required quantity.
o.change_evidence: Property-level evidence from highly automated dormitory, coliving, and workforce-housing operators—especially unattended-site hours, resident self-service rates, emergency response, regulatory staffing requirements, and service-quality incidents—could materially move this estimate.
caveats: Accountability and minimum staffing requirements vary by state, property type, resident population, and contract.
The estimate concerns whether an operator remains required, not whether individual administrative tasks are automated.
Some simple rooming houses may shift more administration to software than full-service dormitories or camps.

narrative.executive_view: This is a small, heterogeneous operating niche built around essential physical accommodation. The credible AI opportunity is concentrated in reservations, billing, resident communications, scheduling, documentation, procurement, and management support; most labor remains cleaning, food preparation, residential presence, and maintenance. Commercially transferable operators exist, but captive dormitories, membership housing, passive property structures, and asset-only transactions materially shrink the relevant population.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can triage resident inquiries, draft and route communications, reconcile charges, support leasing and collections, forecast occupancy, schedule staff, summarize incidents, and surface maintenance priorities. It is less able to replace room cleaning, cooking, repairs, site inspection, de-escalation, emergency response, and the trusted residential-advisor role. The strongest operating design is therefore a centralized digital service layer supporting on-site teams, not an unattended property.
narrative.value_capture: Resident leases and academic-year fixed terms permit savings to remain with the operator until renewal rather than forcing immediate pass-through. Over repeated renewals, market competition and institutional procurement can share the benefit with customers, especially in sponsored worker housing or transparent service contracts. Capture depends on improving labor productivity without degrading responsiveness, safety, cleanliness, or resident experience.
narrative.firm_availability: The classification spans commercially operated rooming and student housing as well as fraternity, sorority, club, and worker-camp structures. Only an estimated portion of the dataset-implied LMM firms are independent, externally serving, staffed platforms whose control can transfer. Transaction evidence specific to this code is absent, and property sales must be separated from acquisition of a continuing operating business.
narrative.demand_durability: Five-year constant-quality demand is likely close to flat with a modest positive base. Postsecondary enrollment projections support the student component, physical housing scarcity supports rooming demand, and project activity can support worker accommodation, but each submarket is local and cyclical. Regardless of volume, occupied accommodation still requires accountable physical operations, so software is more likely to change how the service is delivered than eliminate the service basket.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest risks are classification heterogeneity, an unknown eligible-firm denominator, asset-heavy economics, local occupancy cycles, regulation, resident safety, privacy, fair-housing compliance, and weak integration across older property systems. A bad outcome would combine low eligibility and sparse control transfers with only small administrative savings, while rent competition or employer contracting shares those savings with customers. The frozen compensation ratio also uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a cross-code harmonization, so it may misstate current operating labor intensity.
narrative.principal_driver: A repeatable centralized AI layer can reduce administrative and coordination work across multi-property operators while on-site teams continue the physical service.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code mixes transferable commercial operators with captive, membership, passive-property, and asset-sale structures, leaving firm eligibility and qualifying transfer incidence poorly observed.

sources.title: 721310: Rooming and Boarding Houses, Dormitories, and Workers' Camps - Census Bureau Profile
sources.supports: Defines the included establishment types, principal-residence accommodation, possible housekeeping/meals/laundry services, and reports 1,589 employer establishments in 2023.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 721300
sources.supports: Industry occupation mix and wages: 9,940 total jobs; food service 21.47%, cleaning/grounds 27.21%, residential advisors 13.19%, office/admin 15.22%, management 9.08%, and maintenance/repair 7.25%.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Hotel, Motel, and Resort Desk Clerks
sources.supports: Lists desk-clerk tasks including reservations, room assignment, account records, charges, payments, inquiries, complaint escalation, bookkeeping, key issuance, housekeeping coordination, and staff scheduling.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Maids and Housekeeping Cleaners
sources.supports: Describes the physical cleaning occupation and core tasks such as cleaning rooms and common areas, replenishing supplies, and cleaning carpets and furnishings.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: BTOS evidence that overall business AI use was 17%-20% from December 2025 to May 2026, expected six-month use was 20%-23%, adoption was below 20% for the smallest firms, and use increased with larger size.

sources.title: Generative AI at Work
sources.supports: Field evidence from 5,179 customer-support agents that conversational AI assistance increased issues resolved per hour by 14% on average, with heterogeneous effects by worker experience.

sources.title: Strategic Student & Senior Housing Trust, Inc. 2024 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes a student-housing property leased by the bed on fixed individual terms aligned with the academic year, billed monthly, with furnished units and shared amenities.

sources.title: NCES Table 303.10: Total fall enrollment in degree-granting postsecondary institutions through 2031
sources.supports: Projects total enrollment of 19,807,880 in 2026 and 20,233,776 in 2031, with projections prepared in December 2022.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Lodging Managers
sources.supports: Projects lodging-manager employment to grow 3% from 2024 to 2034 and describes duties involving inspection, standards, resident or guest questions, staff management, front-desk coordination, budgets, and round-the-clock accountability.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report - Market Trends
sources.supports: Reports 2,345 US small businesses bought and sold in 2026 Q1 and explains that its market analysis covers roughly 50,000 businesses for sale and recently sold, with closed transactions voluntarily reported by brokers.

sources.title: ALFRED: Employment for Accommodation and Food Services: Rooming and Boarding Houses (NAICS 721310)
sources.supports: Reports the 2024 employment index at 77.0 with 2017=100 and defines employment to include wage and salary workers, unincorporated self-employed workers, and unpaid family workers.
